1: כְּאַיָּל תַּעֲרֹג עַל-אֲפִיקֵי-מָיִם (kĕʾayyāl taʿărōg ʿal-ʾăpîqê-māyim, "As a deer longs for flowing streams") — ʾayyāl: a male deer/stag (the feminine ʾayyālāh also possible; some texts have the feminine panting; both genders used). ʿĀrag: to long for, to pant (possibly an onomatopoeia of the panting breath; used only here and Joel 1:20 of animals). ʾĂpîqîm: river-channels, watercourses (the irrigation channels of Ps 1:3). כֵּן נַפְשִׁי תַעֲרֹג אֵלֶיךָ אֱלֹהִים (kên napshî taʿărōg ʾêlêkā ʾĕlōhîm, "so my soul longs for you, O God") — the simile equates nāpesh (the animating self, the whole person) with the thirsting deer. The longing is whole-person, not merely intellectual or emotional.
2: צָמְאָה נַפְשִׁי לֵאלֹהִים לְאֵל חָי (ṣāmĕʾāh napshî lêʾlōhîm lĕʾêl ḥāy, "My soul thirsts for God, for the living God") — ṣāmēʾ: to thirst (the physical, urgent thirst of dehydration). ʾÊl ḥāy: the living God — the one who is characterized by life (ḥayyîm), in contrast to the idols who are dead (mêtîm). John 7:37-38 echoes this thirst-language: "If anyone is thirsty, let him come to me and drink." מָתַי אָבוֹא וְאֵרָאֶה פְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים (mātay ʾābôʾ wĕʾêrāʾeh pĕnê ʾĕlōhîm, "When will I come and stand in God's presence?") — literally "see the face of God" — the pilgrim desire to appear before God in the sanctuary (Exod 23:17). The exile from the temple is the exile from the face of God.
3: הָיְתָה-לִּי דִמְעָתִי לֶחֶם יוֹמָם וָלָיְלָה (hāyĕtāh-llî dimʿātî leḥem yômām wālaylāh, "My tears have been my food day and night") — dimʿāh: tears (tear-drops); leḥem: bread, food. The tears replace the bread — grief replaces nourishment. The inversion of the hunger-satisfaction cycle: instead of eating to live, one weeps without end. בֶּאֱמֹר אֵלַי כָּל-הַיּוֹם אַיֵּה אֱלֹהֶיךָ (beʾĕmōr ʾêlay kol-hayyôm ʾayyêh ʾĕlōhêkā, "while people keep saying to me all day long, 'Where is your God?'") — the repeated taunt (ʾayyêh: where?): the enemies interpret the Psalmist's suffering as proof of divine absence or divine abandonment. This is the same taunt in Ps 79:10 and Ps 115:2.
4: אֵלֶּה אֶזְכְּרָה וְאֶשְׁפְּכָה עָלַי נַפְשִׁי (ʾêlleh ʾezkĕrāh wĕʾeshpĕkāh ʿālay napshî, "These things I remember as I pour out my soul") — shāpak: to pour out (as water is poured from a vessel). The nāpesh poured out — the whole self emptied in grief (as in Ps 62:8: "pour out your hearts to him"; and Lam 2:19). כִּי אֶעֱבֹר בַּסָּךְ (kî ʾeʿĕbōr bassāk, "how I used to walk with the crowd, leading them in procession") — sāk: the sheltering canopy of the festival crowd, the festal procession with its protective multitude. The memory of leading the pilgrimage procession to the house of God, now inaccessible.
5: מַה-תִּשְׁתּוֹחֲחִי נַפְשִׁי (māh-tishtôḥăḥî napshî, "Why are you downcast, O my soul?") — the refrain (also v11; 43:5): the Psalmist addresses himself in the second person — a kind of inner dialogue or self-counsel. Shāḥāh (hithpolel: to bow down, to be bent over, to be downcast) — the soul bowed under the weight of grief. וּמַה-תֶּהֱמִי עָלָי (ûmāh-tehĕmî ʿālay, "Why so disturbed within me?") — hāmāh: to roar, to make a loud sound, to be in tumult — the inner noise of distress. יַחֲלִי לֵאלֹהִים (yaḥălî lêʾlōhîm, "Put your hope in God") — yāḥal: to wait with expectation. The self-counsel: direct the tumult toward God rather than turning inward.
6: עָלַי נַפְשִׁי תִשְׁתּוֹחָח (ʿālay napshî tishtôḥāḥ, "My soul is cast down within me") — resuming the self-address: the soul that was told to hope (v5) is still downcast. עַל-כֵּן אֶזְכָּרְךָ מֵאֶרֶץ יַרְדֵּן וְחֶרְמוֹנִים (ʿal-kên ʾezkārĕkā mêʾereṣ yardên wĕḥermônîm, "therefore I remember you from the land of Jordan, the heights of Hermon") — the physical distance from Jerusalem specified: the Jordan region and Hermon (the north) — far from the temple, recalling God from a distance. Memory as the substitute for physical presence when access is denied.
7: תְּהוֹם-אֶל-תְּהוֹם קוֹרֵא (tĕhôm-ʾel-tĕhôm qôrêʾ, "Deep calls to deep") — tĕhôm: the primordial deep (Gen 1:2: "the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the tĕhôm"). The deep calls to the deep — disaster summons disaster in a chain reaction of overwhelming force. לְקוֹל צִנּוֹרֶיךָ (lĕqôl ṣinnôrêkā, "at the roar of your waterfalls") — ṣinnôr: a waterspout, a channel (possibly the torrential channels through which floodwaters pour). כָּל-מִשְׁבָּרֶיךָ וְגַלֶּיךָ עָלַי עָבָרוּ (kol-mishbārêkā wĕgallêkā ʿālay ʿābārû, "all your waves and breakers have rolled over me") — mishbār: a breaker (where waves break on rocks); gal: a wave. Both owned by "you" — these are God's waves, not random chaos. The same language Jonah uses in his prayer from the fish's belly (Jon 2:3).
8: יוֹמָם יְצַוֶּה יְהוָה חַסְדּוֹ (yômām yĕṣawweh YHWH ḥasdô, "By day the LORD directs his steadfast love") — the counter-movement within the flood: while the depths overwhelm, by day the LORD commands (ṣāwāh: to command, to issue an order) his ḥesed (covenant loyalty). וּבַלַּיְלָה שִׁירֹה עִמִּי (ûbalaylāh shîrô ʿimmî, "at night his song is with me") — even through the night of suffering, the divine song (shîr) accompanies. תְּפִלָּה לְאֵל חַיַּי (tĕpillāh lĕʾêl ḥayyāy, "a prayer to the God of my life") — ʾêl ḥayyāy: the God of my life/lives — the same epithet as v2 (ʾêl ḥāy), now possessively attached: "my" life-God.
9: אוֹמְרָה לְאֵל סַלְעִי לָמָה שְׁכַחְתָּנִי (ʾômĕrāh lĕʾêl salʿî lāmāh shĕkahttānî, "I say to God my rock, 'Why have you forgotten me?'") — selaʿ: the rock/cliff-face (as in Ps 18:2; 31:3). לָמָּה-קֹדֵר אֵלֵךְ (lāmmāh-qōdêr ʾêlêk, "Why do I walk in mourning?") — qādar: to be dark, to mourn (from the darkened appearance of the mourner's face). בְּלַחַץ אוֹיֵב (bĕlaḥaṣ ʾôyêb, "under the oppression of the enemy") — laḥaṣ: pressure, oppression (from lāḥaṣ: to press, to squeeze). The enemy as a force that presses and constricts.
10: בְּרֶצַח בְּעַצְמוֹתַי חֵרְפוּנִי (bĕreṣaḥ bĕʿaṣmôtay ḥêrĕpûnî, "As if a sword were cutting through my bones, my enemies taunt me") — reṣaḥ: a killing blow, a shattering stroke (from rāṣaḥ: to murder, to slay). The taunt is experienced as a physical blow to the skeleton. Ḥārap: to taunt, to reproach, to shame (the same word as ḥerpāh in Ps 31:11). בְּאָמְרָם אֵלַי כָּל-הַיּוֹם אַיֵּה אֱלֹהֶיךָ — the same taunt as v3: "Where is your God?" — the repetition showing the relentless nature of the social assault.
11: The refrain for the second time: מַה-תִּשְׁתּוֹחֲחִי נַפְשִׁי — "Why are you downcast, O my soul?" The repetition of the identical refrain (also in 43:5) argues strongly that Psalms 42 and 43 are one literary unit. The refrain is not despair — it is the Psalmist's deliberate self-interruption, refusing to let grief have the final word: יַחֲלִי לֵאלֹהִים כִּי-עוֹד אוֹדֶנּוּ יְשׁוּעֹת פָּנָיו (yaḥălî lêʾlōhîm kî-ʿôd ʾôdennû yĕshûʿōt pānāyw, "Put your hope in God, for I will yet praise him — my Savior and my God") — yĕshûʿōt pānāyw: "salvations of his face" — the face as the source of saving presence.
